sentences.text: Hey.
Hey, how's it going?
Good.
Alright… Good to see you, have a nice long weekend.
Yeah, did nothing, but it was good.
What about you?
Pretty much the same, to be honest.
Nice.
So, I think Alec and Trent will be joining from our side.
Okay.
And then we had 5 confirms from our living.
So, and I know we covered a good amount of this with them.
early August, so… Yeah.
But I don't think with this group, I know, Melanie and Tony have seen it if they join.
Yeah.
So, anyway, I guess, where are we at with our standard renewal?
They signed, they renewed.
They did.
Okay, awesome.
I'm gonna go get water, sorry.
Yeah, no worries.
Okay.
Hey guys.
Hey, Charlotte, how are you doing?
Hello!
Ed, how are you?
Pretty good.
It's a Tuesday that feels like a Monday, so…
I don't know if that's, like… I think that's a win, right?
Short week?
I think so.
Yeah.
How's your weekend?
On our end, we might have another guy joining, but we can go ahead and get started, for the time being.
Okay, sounds good.
And I know, Tony, we had met with Tony and Melanie on this in early August.
And I know that they wanted you all to get your eyes on it, as you'd be probably the primary users of the tool.
So I did my research, LinkedIn, always do, but, would love just as a good starting point, to do some introductions.
Can start from our side, and then if you wouldn't mind just letting us know who's on the call, that'd be wonderful.
Yep.
I'm talking, so I'll go first.
I'm, Matthew, Senior Director here with IQ.
I've been working with… Our living for probably around 3 years now, and just recently was introduced to the, Reef, side of the table.
Hey y'all, I'm Larissa, I'm RR Living's, Client Success Manager here at Apartment IQ, and I've been on the account, I think I'm going on a year and a half now.
It's nice to meet you all.
Hey everybody, my name is Trent Riddle, this is my second week here at Apartment IQ, so learning along with everyone else, but excited to be talking to you guys today.
I'm on the product team, so…
Awesome.

So, I've been with Reef for about a year and a half now, and, me, as well as Ryan Perry on the call, we are both on the acquisitions team, so, you know, I know, obviously, our living and the asset managers are super informed of all of ApartmentIQ's capabilities, and our team, you know, uses it, but always looking for more ways to incorporate it into what we do.
Absolutely, and I guess, Charlotte, as a starting point, what is your familiarity with the tool?
Where are you spending the most time right now?

There's obviously some new product that we'll review today that's probably more in line with a CoStar or a broader market dynamic type of tool, but I'm interested to hear just directly from you right now.
How you're using IQ, really, if at all.
Yeah, I guess, you know, in our underwriting and deals, we really use it for, like, comp analysis and stuff.
Obviously, we use it hand-in-hand with CoStar, but yeah, market trends, comps, things, you know, pulling property data for our properties to compare as well.
But yeah, hand-in-hand with, you know, CoStar's capabilities.
Okay, and when you're coming in, I guess, are you coming in with comps in mind, or are you using the, like, the ranking algorithm that exists in IQ to, start to inform that decision?
We'll do both, yeah.
Okay, and then, just, like, more broadly, how are you usually, like, benchmarking potential acquisition against the broader market?
What's, I guess, what's important to you, outside of just comps?
What data points are you looking at, and what's that process look like today?
I mean, everything that we look at towards comps is mostly rent-driven.
It's more available data, and then, you know, accumulating our properties on the expenses side, geographically.
Awesome.
Makes sense.
Well, obviously you know ApartmentIQ, so the main product that we'll focus on today is Explore.

This is a newer tool as of last quarter, and essentially, it's taking the same foundation of ApartmentIQ in terms of the, near real-time pricing and availability, and it's scaling that into, full market intelligence in now, forecasting.
So… In addition to our collection method of… Visiting property websites every evening from 10 p.
m.
Eastern until 5 a.
m.
Eastern the following morning, and gathering about 35 million units of data.
We've layered in census information, ACS, down to the individual census tract.
For, demographics, as well as, building permits and government database information for supply.
And most recently, we partnered with Real Capital Analytics, if you're familiar with their data or have used them now or in the past.
So, that's what you'll see within the tool as we get into it.
I guess that would be my cue to show you the actual platform.
Okay, so just as a… just as a refresher, just to set the table, really four sections for navigation within ApartmentIQ as it exists today.
First of which is this My Property section.
So this is what your team is using, operationally on existing assets.
Second and probably most popular section of the tool now is this, research functionality.
So this is the ability to search any… property that is non-owned or non-managed, and create the same set of reports that you're getting on your existing portfolio.
It can also be used in a development use case to custom create a subject property to pull in that same set of reports.
Down here, you'll see that we actually released a revenue management tool.
This is powered by all public data.
This is informed by about 16 million units of lease data, and it's interesting in the sense that it uses explainable AI.
Which is newer to the space.
So, in addition to giving the pricing suggestions, we're gonna tell you why that suggestion is being given.
Primary focus today will be… Explore.
So… Let me just get set here, and…
we will get on into… so, I'm gonna start at the, Dallas-Fort Worth…
MSA level as an example, I believe ours based there.
As is Larissa.
And… okay, so what you'll see here is, as a starting point, we're gonna give you a market summary.
So, Right here, you'll see a trailing 12-month, rent growth, occupancy, days on market, and, year-over-year rent growth, and a price per unit.
So, as I click into this rent-to-performance within Dallas-Fort Worth, we've got Ryan joining.
Two Ryans.
Give me a sec here.
Hey, Ryan, how are you doing?
Good, how are you?
I am doing well.
We're just, we're just getting into things here.
Sounds good, sorry for joining late.
Not… not a problem at all.
Got a bit of background.

So, what we're looking at here, market summary, first thing I'll actually call out within the Explorer tool is that, we're starting at an MSA level, but you can really go to any geographical boundary that you would like.
So.
Msa down to zip code or individual neighborhood.
You'll also notice here on the right-hand side that There's draw functionality, so if you want a polygon, radius, or freehand, again, all this data will adjust to the boundary that you have set.
And being that the ACS data is down to the individual census tract.
Iq is really unique in the sense that the demographic information, can be pretty finely curated to the area that you're looking at versus a broader, broader scope.
So… First kind of call-out I'll have there, and then the second is, this filtering system here is going to allow you to look at just the inventory, that's important to you.
So I'll show you an example of that shortly, but starting with the market summary, we're going to give you some, historical information.
As well as, forecasted rents, which I'll speak to in a second.
Also gonna give you a 30-day market snapshot.
So, current state of the market, what are rents, what are concessions, how long are, units sitting on market for.
In this Dallas example.
You can see that, rent growth is down, and the, occupancy rate… well, rent growth has held fairly steady.
Well, occupancy has dipped.

So, as you're starting to look at the market, suggested operators might be holding price, but likely are using, concessions to preserve some occupancy, so… You can see relationally how some of this starts to play together.
In addition to current state of the market and historical performance.
You'll note this supply information here.
So this is what's currently being sourced from building permits, government databases, but functionally what you have here is a history of supply.
And previously constructed, as well as what's coming on market in the coming several years.
So, each one of these, Pieces of inventory will give you a summary of what that project is, and when it's meant to deliver, as well as the, unit size, or the number of units at that project.
So, two brackets here, currently under construction, and scheduled construction.
Scheduled construction being, permit has been accepted, but, hasn't broken ground yet.

Just looking at what you pulled up, is there an ability to… filter those results, like, based on unit count, because obviously for what we're doing, the 6-unit, you know, mini duplexes, or whatever you want to call them, aren't going to be super applicable.
Yeah, there is.
So within this filtering here, you could… if you just wanted to look at new supply, for instance, you could toggle this to just under construction.
And there's a variety of presets, some of which will only be applicable to existing inventory, but in that new construction sense, you can look at your presets here.
So, total units here, if you're going 150 units plus.
Obviously, it's down to your investment thesis as you're looking at this broader market, you know, what you want to see.
And that's the same for both existing and for new supply.
Awesome, thanks.
Yeah, of course
Moving along from supplier, you're gonna have your, summary of demographics in your market, so…
Four buckets here.
You have your population, education, income and employment.
These are, 2024 figures, and again, this is U.
S.
Census, ACS, and the Bureau of Labor.
So I will actually take an example here.
I think it's, like, closest I've gone before is, like, a…
Two-block radius, but again, fairly unique to…
IQ, in that, all of this is… Specific to that, area that's selected.
So, one of a pretty big differentiator, not just taking broad market averages, hyperlocal view, exact renter profile, around that.
subject property, so…
You can do it this way, or you could actually select a subject property and have it, radius out from there.
So, one of the things that we've been super interested in, and I'm curious if y'all have this, like, property-level information, but…

you know, when we look at CoStar, one of the things we struggle with is the fact that all of the unit rents, or, like, one bed, two-bed, they're all, like, blended together, whether or not it's a classic, it's a partial renovation, it's a renovation.
Yep.
Are y'all able to capture that level of granularity when y'all, You know, pull the… the property level rent.
Yeah, we are.
So the, property level rents, you actually get down to the individual unit level.
So, and Alex Slocum just, just joined as well.
He is the, CEO of IQ in Rentable, so, Just wanted to let y'all know he's here.
Hey, Alec.
Right?
I'm like…
All good.
All good.

Ryan, to get back to your question, so… at each, property within IQ, Just again, to kind of, like, set the table for this, we're gathering this information every single night down to the individual unit level.
So, it's from 10 p.
m.
Eastern until 5 a.
m.
Eastern the following morning.
Each individual unit is being tracked and updated for you.
So if I take this, like, unit 1419, for example, pick this unit up 104 days ago.
The gross rent, the concession that's tied to it and that effective is updated every single day.
That's then rolled into, floor plan level information, and then unit type information.
And we do have about 5 years of historical data, so any unit that's ever leased, we have that same amount of data for.
And so, effectively, each property has a reconstructed rent roll that has each individual unit, floor plan, and unit type at that property.
So, so other… could you pull that back up?
Yeah.
Is there, like… because I'm looking at unit… Maybe go back to all units again?
Yeah, it was… it was 1,009 and then 1,010.
They're both the same floor plan, but…
Yep.
There's a…
just call it $250 delta between the rent.
Is there… I assume that's the difference between a renovated unit and a classic unit.
Is there any… Go ahead.
Nope, sorry, you go ahead.
Is there, like, any way for us to tell that, or be able to break out You know, what's renovated from what's not, other than just making assumptions based on rent.
There is.
So, in this case, it actually looks like those units, there's a year gap in between, so you can see the, date here.

But, in… and I'll go back to explore and kind of tie this all together for you, but… Within this My Properties and Research section, which you'll directly be, directly be able to access out of Explore, you do have a set of filters here.

So, if you're looking particularly at unit-style renovated versus classics, this wouldn't play well right now because it's Nashville, it's a ton of new supply, but you do have the ability to, distinguish between units.
Bye.
Okay.
Even more so if there's particular units that you're looking at.
We do track unit-specific amenities as well.
So, you'll see here if there's particular amenities or features of a unit that might be driving renter decision, that's called out.
Things like the concession details tied to that unit, or even best lease length, is called out as well.
Cool.
So just to kind of go back into the flow, say you're looking in, Dallas.
Sorry, Matt, I just want one more thing to add there, Ryan.
So what we're looking at on the website is we're looking for the floor plan and the unit names that have some kind of an indication that the unit, or the floor plan's been renovated.
How often do you find that that's exhaustive of all the units you're looking for, or is there somewhere else that you'd want us to be tying that back?

Probably about 50-50, because sometimes, you know, we'll go to websites, and there is the… it'll say gold, or platinum, or whatever you want to call it, and then sometimes it's just, you know, this is our 750 square foot unit, and You really have to dig into, like, the amenity drop-down if you want to see what's the difference between the one that's renting for $1,500 and the one that's renting for $1,700.
So, as much as that could be, like, simplified for us, that's kind of… You know, what we want to see.
Got it.
And you end up having to go to websites directly right now, I imagine, to suss that out?
Websites directly, and then, you know, sometimes it even comes down to us calling and, trying to collect the data ourselves.
Yep.
So, We've got our… we've got a research, like, a data science team that's planning to pick this up in the next 30 days.
For this specific use case.
So I think we'll have updates to be able to share there, and then would love to give you a sense of how things are looking and get your feedback on it along the way.
Yeah, that'd be awesome.
Okay.
Hey, Melanie, how are you doing?
Hey, I'm good.
I just got off another call, so I thought I'd jump over here and see if y'all needed anything, or anything like that.
Well, we're here to serve you.
I appreciate you taking some time.
Well, you're in good hands with Ryan being able to explain it all, for sure.
That sounds good.
Good conversation so far.
While you're here, Melanie, this is Alec, the CEO of Rentable and ApartmentIQ, so…
Well, hello, Alec.
Nice to see you, Mellie.
Nice to meet you.
Awesome.
So, I don't know, Alec, if you want to take over, you want me to continue with the demo, but, we had just gone through market summary.
I think probably the next kind of natural step here is, Ryan, if you have a particular, market or investment thesis that you typically look at.
One of the really unique things about Explore is that it can take you from market overview all the way down to an individual property.
And from there, actually generate one of those research reports, a comp set, to directly assist with that underwriting process.
Yeah, let me take a look really quick.
We're looking at a deal right now in Houston called 2828 at Royal Oaks, so… If we could go super high level, and then…
Yeah, kind of nailed down.
on that.
you said 2828 Royal Oaks, so you could either do it direct out of that property, and you can actually radius from here.
Or if you wanted to go broader Houston and kind of drill down to that, you'd be able to do it that way as well.
So, you'll see here, right, if I go back to the market summary…
Here's gonna be your rented performance, that 30-day snapshot that we reviewed.
Scheduled construction in the area, and then your demographic information.
Would you be able to give me a bit more information about 2828 in the sense of, how many units it is, class of property, etc?
Yeah, it's like a class.
B-, it's 510 units, it's a… gonna be a value add.
Matt, are you, are you… looks like you're on app right now?
If you switch to Alpha, you'll be able to see the property detail page specifically for 2828.
At Royal Oaks.
Like, directly out of here.
Yeah, it should pop right up, actually, when you search the address.
Oh, gotcha.
I don't know if I have access to health.
You want me to shift it?
You wanna pick it up?
Yeah, sure, one sec.
Okay.
Just wanna make sure that he's seeing the most.
Up to date…
Alright.
So, 2828 at Royal.
Folks… Hayes Road?
Yep.
That's it.
So this is the new… maybe Matt already walked you through the property detail page, but this is a new view of properties that we've integrated into Explorer.
So, a few things that you're gonna notice that are gonna be different.
One, we've brought in images and photos of these properties.

And these are coming directly, actually, from… we have an ILS that does integrations with all… with a lot of, properties throughout the country, and so we pull in those images from there, so if you ever want to see everything about a property that we're pulling in, you can always come in and get a sense of what they've got online.
To get a sense of that property.

And then going back here, there's going to be some new data sets, so it's going to be a combination of what you're used to seeing, which is real-time or daily pricing, availability, and performance of the property, but also you're going to start to see ownership group, data, last transaction data, which we can show you more of below.
And this is all coming from a partnership we have with a company called Real Capital Analytics, which maybe you all know or have already talked about with Matt.
Yep.
Perfect.
So, Matt, did you walk through this page already?
I ha- I haven't.
Okay.
So, we got through, we essentially got high-level market summary, we were going there, going there next.
Okay, got it.
Well, maybe I'll just… I'll just walk you through this page.
Is there any… anything in particular that you all are… are wondering about or looking at for this property, Ryan?
Oh, well, one thing, just starting off, I noticed if you scroll up a little bit, y'all had a sales, price.
Wondering where y'all, source those, especially in, you know, non-disclosure states like Texas.
Yeah, so we work with a partner called RCA, Real Capital Analytics.
And they're the ones that power the transaction history.
Our understanding… we did a ton of due diligence on potential providers here, and they were the best by a lot.
I'd have to go back and talk to them about how they specifically sourced this deal.
But I know it's a combination of public records.
Private conversations, usually for every data point, they've got at least two sources that they're using.
Cool.
And more, like, on the… The property level, like, stuff we'd like to see.

This is kind of a mismanaged asset, so I'd love to see, like, how, you know, rents have changed over the past 12 months, what concessions they've had to give, how vacancy has, changed over that same period of time.
Yeah.
So this first, you can see a chart that shows over time, and then you can also show the… see the actual pricing and availability that's happened for this property.
So in this case, we're looking at their rent for the last year.
We could take it further back if you'd like.
And you can see rent, net effective rent, and on a per square foot basis for both.
So, and then it's comparing them directly to their… the comp set.
That we automatically pull, and then the market that it's within.
And so you can see a decent amount of variance here, and I'm guessing that's concessions that are driving that, so if I look at concessions as a percentage of rent.
Looks like a big spike last fall, got all the way up to 25%.

Came back down, looks like the comp set does have a decent, you know, pretty high level of… As well, but then, looks like more recently, the comp sets, kind of, sort of, looks like in Q2, stuck around 6.
2%, they popped back up to almost 17%.
As a percentage of rent for concessions.
And now there's… they're settling back in, it looks like around 8 since July.
Maybe optimizing that.
T90 Red Roll.
And then on the occupancy side.
Here's what we're seeing for occupancy.
So, down 85%.
Last October.
Got a little spike in the spring, trying to make a little bit of progress here from what we're seeing.
So, is this based on… I know it says advertised occupancy.
One thing about this deal that, like.
may be causing the information not to be correct, because it is about 70% occupied right now, is they have… 15% of their units are down currently.
Yep.
Do you think that's what's, like, contributing to this?
That's definitely what it is, yeah.
This is… this is using all the ApartmentIQ data, so it's all publicly available data.
So down units, we wouldn't see.
Yeah, if they're not… Ryan, if they're not showing them in their system available for rent, it won't pick them up.
Okay.
It's interesting you call that out, Ryan.

If you look… I'm just looking at the history, you can kind of click out from this page and do an actual research report and do more diligence on the deal, and it looks like in March of last year, they started to hide a lot of units, and there's a lot of availability up until a couple weeks ago, and then they kind of hid almost everything, it looks like, so… That's what would cause the fluctuations in that data.
Yeah, I was just noticing, so this is their exposure.
So, you can see their exposure goes all the way up to 25% here, so that's probably right in line with that occupancy you're seeing.
And then, yeah, they probably started hiding units in whatever, you know, whenever they started marketing the product, so… August.
It looks like just a couple weeks ago.
Yeah, and if you go to their property website, you'll see they're showing, like, two of each unit type, essentially.
Yeah, there's their exposure.

So, what our system will do, we haven't brought it into this section yet, but what our system will do when we see something like this, typically, is flag it and say, okay, we think they're hiding availability.
But you can obviously use their historicals to get a sense here for this property of what the actual is.
Yeah, this is all very good.
useful data.
So… Can you show, like, every… I guess if you go to Rent Roll, like, can you see each individual floor plan?
Rather than just, like, a kind of rolled up one bed, one bath, like you have there.
Yep, so if you want to drive into the floor plan level, so this first section, this is the pricing availability, which you can change the time frame on.
So this is every unit that we've seen come available, on whatever time frame you're looking at.
How many of them leased, and you can drill in for each, you can drill into the units themselves.
So you can always see What came on, what came off, what the lease terms were that were the best lease terms, so what are they really optimizing for with their revenue management?
How long they sat on market for.
What those concessions looked like.
So this is just what's come on in the last, you know, you can push up to a year, here, and start to see what those trends look like on a floor plan basis as well.
And then on the rent roll side, you can actually drill in and see this is every unit we've ever seen on this property.
So you can come in and actually drill in and see
every single unit.

And it sounds like it would be helpful for you to be able to roll this up to… Similar to this pricing availability table, it'd be helpful to roll this up to a floor plan level to be able to see every unit we've seen for every floor plan.
Yeah, if you could just match it by square footage, that'd be very useful.
Cut.
Yeah, and so we've started to put in some different logic here, too, for this estimated rent roll.
So we've… we're showing the leased rent across everything, and so if you drill into the actual units themselves.
This is a very big one, so I'll take one that's slightly smaller.
But for each unit, you could sort by square footage if you really wanted to.
Looks like these are all the same anyway.
And then you can see, what did the unit rent at?
And win.
And then, what do we estimate the market rent is today?
And you can calculate that loss to lease from that.
And the way we're calculating market rent is we're looking at the last 5 leases.
on that bed group level, and we're controlling, and we're looking at a per-square-foot basis to be able to roll that into the market rent.
So it should be a pretty close estimate of what the property's actually achieving.
Okay.
Cool.
This will also show you how long has it been since we've last seen this unit, so the functional estimated resident tenure, how long have these These renters all been there.
And does that roll up into, like, an average somewhere?
Where you can pull, like, you know, your average turnover, or average, you know, resident.
Stay.
We've got the data, I don't think we're doing it at this level right now, but, it'd be really easy to add.
Yeah, that's, especially as we… budget for expenses on the turn side.
That could be super useful.
Okay.
Got it.
Now, Ryan, where are you getting this information now?
Are you just going on CoStar, or…
As far as, like, rent?
Yeah.
So this was a conversation we had at the beginning of the call, but we started with CoStar, but the issue there is that the blended rents between classics and renovated.
Yep.
You can kind of skew it, whether or not you're…
20% renovated or 100% renovated.

So, recently, we've had, Ryan and some of our team members just calling places, and You know, asking, hey, you know, we… just surveying what the different rents are, and asking a lot about their renovation scope, and… The different costs for each unit.
Okay.
Yeah, I didn't think you were… you had a system that you were using anymore.
Yeah, so something like this would be extremely, useful.
Yeah, we really like it.
We really like their product.
So, Ryan, just to clarify on that, so you're saying at that rent roll level, a delineation of is it renovated or not would be useful for your analysis.
Is that at a high level, what you're asking?
Yes, and I know that's, like.

kind of difficult to pinhole into, like, one thing, because you… if a deal is built in the, you know, 1990, you're gonna have 5 different levels of renovation, but Just to, like, the best of… y'all's… Ability with the information that y'all have.
Finding a way to clearly report, like.
What level of finishes each unit has, and then the associated.
Hmm.
That makes sense.
I think we could do a lot of work on that one, for you.
And actually, I, Ryan, I did miss this.
Actually, there's a renewal rate And that's the renewal rate right here for you, so it's just, right next to exposure, lease, and occupancy.
Sure.
Awesome.
And this is just what we're looking at as we're looking at in the last 12 months.
You know, we know that, we estimate there's about 96 total units.
How many have we seen actually come on the market in the last 96 months?
Or, sorry.
Hmm.
Of those 96 in the last 12 months.

Yeah, so that renovation, and it sounds like, Ryan, being able to assign a value to the renovated, like, what actually got renovated, in what unit, and then would it be ideal to just be able to sort of toggle this rent roll by renovated versus non-renovated, or what's the… What's the interface that would make that comparison most easy for you?
Yeah, I guess if there was maybe a toggle where you could do, like, blended versus renovated versus classic…
to be able to break it down to renovated versus not.
But you might get into challenges with some of the properties have so many different floor plans, it might be difficult, and you might not know what is renovated or what wouldn't.
Agreed.
Yeah, that could be an issue.
Yeah, because a lot of these properties will have 3 or 4 iterations of what they call a renovated.
And you'd have to have the nomenclature of their P and Q, or whatever it is.
Yeah, so we see, you know, we're capturing all that data from the naming.

And so we could enable you all… I mean, we're gonna do our best to figure out and get all those synonyms right, but we could functionally give you all the ability to say, hey, for this property, if they've got a queue after the floor plan name, that's a renovated unit, we could tag it based on that if you want.
And the other thing, there's some other data points that we do see, so we can see images.
If they have unit-level imagery, we can use AI to look at those and help, guide what that looks like.

And the other thing we can look at is just price differences, you know, so if it's the same square footage, if it's basically the same floor, we don't think it's amenity differences causing the price difference.
You know, usually you want to see that $50 to $150 increase in rents as a result of the renovation, depending on the scale.
And so we can start to infer the likelihood that a unit's renovated from there as well, and that's a lot of the work that we're starting to do now.
Bye.
That's good.
And then one more clarification, Ryan.
You said you wanted to see the average lease link at the unit level on the rent roll?
Is that also… Accurate.
Yeah, that… that's kind of paired with the renewal percentage, I guess.
Yeah.
Really just…
trying to budget on the expense side, you know, how many units we're going to have turning over each year, would be the most useful for us.
So I think that renewal percentage Gives us what we need.
